coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of carbon dots from Nigella sativa seeds for supercapacitor application
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ugwuoke, Cyril O.
Ghali, Mohsen
El-Moneim, Ahmed Abd
coredata.dc:description: The synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) from sources such as coal and phenolic resins requires the use of chemicals that sometimes are hazardous to the environment. Several studies have highlighted the significance of using natural and biomass sources in ecologically friendly CDs synthesis. This strategy guarantees their accessibility, environmental friendliness and cost-effectiveness. In this work, we report the synthesis of CDs from Nigella sativa seeds for supercapacitor application. A hydrothermal technique was adopted for this experiment. The structural, size distribution, fluorescence, and electrochemical properties of the synthesized CDs were studied using an X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectroscope, Transition electron microscope (TEM), spectrofluorometer and potentiostat, respectively. The TEM revealed distinct scattered quasi-spherical dots on the surface and the estimated size distribution ranges from 6.08 to 9.91 nm. The fluorescence spectra revealed that CDs have green light emission, which redshifts as the excitation wavelength increases. The electrochemical measurement confirms that CDs exhibited an electrical double layer (EDL) capacitance behavior with an areal capacitance of 2.82 mF/cm2 at 5.21 μA/cm2 current density. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on the fabrication of supercapacitor from CD derived from Nigella sativa seeds.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nigella sativa seeds
Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Quasi-spherical dots
PVA/H2SO4
Supercapacitors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram illustrating the synthesis procedure of CD.
The picture of (a) LIG encrypted on a Kapton paper (b) deposited samples on LIG (i) LIG + CD-160 (j) LIG + CD-200 and (k) LIG + CD-240.
The XRD patterns of CDs prepared at various temperatures.
The TEM micrograph of (a) CD-160 (b) CD-200 (c) CD-240 and their respective estimated size.
FTIR spectra of CDs indicating all containing functional groups.
The XPS spectra of CD-240 (a) survey (b) C 1s (c) O 1s (d) N 1s

The plot of (a) CD-160 PL emission, (b) CD-200 PL emission, (c) CD-240 PL emission, and (d) before and after excitation of the samples (from left to right CD-160, CD-200 and CD-240) (e) CDs absorbance.
The CV curve of (a) LIG (b) LIG + CD-160 (c) LIG + CD-200 (d) LIG + CD-240 (e) the comparison of the four samples' CV at 100 mV/s scan rate and (f) the area under (e).
The plot of log i against log v.
The GCD curve of (a) LIG (b) LIG + CD-160 (c) LIG + CD-200 (d) LIG + CD-240 (e) the comparison of the four samples at 18.22 μA/cm2.
The plots of areal capacitance against current density and volumetric capacitance against current density.
(a) The EIS curve of all the samples. Fitted plots of (b) LIG (c) LIG + CD-160 (d) LIG + CD-200 (e) LIG + CD-240 using EIS spectrum analyzer.
The summary of zeta potential values of CDs.
The comparison of the CD-based SC performance.
The comparison of the LIG + CD-240 based SC performance with other SC carbon-based electrodes.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of carbon dots from Nigella sativa seeds for supercapacitor application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Basic and Applied Science, Egypt-Japan University of Science and Technology, Alexandria, Egypt

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Basic and Applied Science
Egypt-Japan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Kafrelsheikh University, Kafrelsheikh, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Kafrelsheikh University, Kafrelsheikh, Egypt

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Institute of Basic and Applied Science, Egypt-Japan University of Science and Technology, Alexandria, Egypt.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Basic and Applied Science
Egypt-Japan University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) from sources such as coal and phenolic resins requires the use of chemicals that sometimes are hazardous to the environment. Several studies have highlighted the significance of using natural and biomass sources in ecologically friendly CDs synthesis. This strategy guarantees their accessibility, environmental friendliness and cost-effectiveness. In this work, we report the synthesis of CDs from Nigella sativa seeds for supercapacitor application. A hydrothermal technique was adopted for this experiment. The structural, size distribution, fluorescence, and electrochemical properties of the synthesized CDs were studied using an X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectroscope, Transition electron microscope (TEM), spectrofluorometer and potentiostat, respectively. The TEM revealed distinct scattered quasi-spherical dots on the surface and the estimated size distribution ranges from 6.08 to 9.91 nm. The fluorescence spectra revealed that CDs have green light emission, which redshifts as the excitation wavelength increases. The electrochemical measurement confirms that CDs exhibited an electrical double layer (EDL) capacitance behavior with an areal capacitance of 2.82 mF/cm2 at 5.21 μA/cm2 current density. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on the fabrication of supercapacitor from CD derived from Nigella sativa seeds.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to the rapid increase in the world population and industries, there would be no slack in global energy consumption. Despite the well-known environmental damage that fossil fuels like coal, natural gas, and crude oil produce, they have remained the primary and dominant energy sources. In contrast to the past, there is a favorable trend towards the use of greener energy sources like solar, wind, and nuclear power [1–6]. Renewable energy sources have a lot of drawbacks, such as the intermittent nature of the sun and wind, which makes them unreliable. This limitation can be curbed by introducing electrochemical energy storage in the system. It helps to store the harvested energy and supplies it when needed. In addition, energy storage devices may help with the development of smart grid infrastructure, facilitate peak shaving (which lowers peak demand periods), and enable load shifting (which optimizes energy use) [7].

There are many different methods of energy conversion and storage that are currently being used, including electrochemical batteries, chemical energy storage (such as methane, ammonia and hydrogen), thermochemical energy storage, flywheel energy storage, supercapacitors, and hybrid systems [8–10] – supercapacitors (SCs) and batteries are most outstanding. Traditionally, supercapacitors mainly use liquid electrolytes and they provide excellent ionic conductivity and close interfacial across the electrolyte and electrodes [11,12]. The liquid electrolyte is sometimes considered unsafe due to its limitations, which include high dendrite formation, toxicity, volatility, flammability, and caustic [13]. Additionally, because of their limited operating voltage range, liquid electrolytes are considered unsuitable for applications requiring high energy density [14]. This sometimes limits the commercial utility of SCs. To alleviate/improve this issue, new SCs that use polymer-based electrolytes have emerged. Due to their remarkable flexibility, polymer-based electrolytes are regarded as the optimal flexible interface for solid electrolytes. They offer a solution for the high impedance that inflexible solid-solid connections experience [15]. Solid and gel electrolytes are the two main categories of polymer electrolytes. The major drawback of solid polymer electrolytes is the low ionic conductivity at ambient temperature, which limits the SC's performance [16]. The features of both solid and liquid electrolytes are combined in gel-polymer electrolytes, which have better ionic conductivity and a wider spectrum of electrochemical operation [17]. As a result, scientists are paying more attention to how gel-polymer electrolytes interact with electrodes and how well they can absorb liquids. Significant improvements in the usage of polymer electrolytes electrochemical storage systems are the result of the efforts.

Since the discovery of carbon dots (CDs), it has attracted significant interest from researchers. Strong chemical stability, high biocompatibility, photoluminescence (PL), water solubility, low toxicity, affordability, and ease of production and modification are just a few of the inspiring properties of CDs [18,19]. The top-down and the bottom-up approach are typically employed to produce CDs [20]. Simple procedures and various techniques, including hydrothermal treatment, electrochemical oxidation, laser ablation, and microwaves, can be used to synthesize CDs. Among these methods, hydrothermal synthesis is particularly straightforward for synthesizing CDs. It is economical, needs simple tools, consumes little energy, and is user-friendly [21]. Several studies have highlighted the significance of using natural and biomass sources in ecologically friendly CDs synthesis. This strategy guarantees accessibility, environmental friendliness, non-hazardousness, and economic friendliness. Black cumin or black seed, also known as Nigella sativa, is known to have outstanding properties because it contains a sizable quantity of carbon as a naturally occurring chemical molecule [22,23]. The majority of the carbon in Nigella sativa is found in the seeds' numerous organic compounds and structures. These organic substances are an integral part of the phytochemical content of the plant and are crucial for both growth and metabolism [24,25]. Carbohydrates (e.g., starch and sugar), lipids (fat and oil), proteins, and other organic components like alkaloids, flavonoids, and terpenoids are the main carbon-containing substances present in Nigella sativa seeds [26–29]. These naturally occurring carbon contents are extracted, modified, and structured, forming the CDs. The complex organic molecules are carbon atoms bound to other elements like oxygen, nitrogen, and hydrogen. These elements play a huge role in enhancing the properties of CDs derived from Nigella sativa seeds. The abundant oxygen and nitrogen in biomass helps to improve the electrochemical performance of the formed materials [30–32]. CDs derived from Nigella sativa seeds have been reported to be used in a few applications, such as bioimaging and sensing. N. Sharma and K. Yun [16] reported the sensing of tetracycline and l-Lysine by CDs synthesized from Nigella sativa seeds. Similarly, H. Ehtesabi and F. Massah [33] reported the sensing application of CDs from Nigella sativa seeds. They used the change in the PL emission to sense the presence of acetone. H. Al-Karagoly et al. [34] reported the use of Nigella sativa seed extract as an effective capping agent in iron oxide nanoparticles for antimicrobial activity. Even though the synthesis of CDs derived from Nigella sativa seeds has been utilized for sensing applications, there is still a need to explore other potential applications such as electrochemical energy storage systems.

In this work, we report a one-pot hydrothermal synthesis of CDs from Nigella sativa seeds, which was used to fabricate SC. To the best of our knowledge, this would be the first time reporting a supercapacitor fabrication from CDs derived from Nigella sativa seeds.

The materials used in this experiment include Nigella sativa seeds, deionized (DI) water, and Kapton paper. Fig. 1 shows the synthesis procedure of CDs using a hydrothermal technique. The Nigella sativa seeds were first crushed to form a fine black powder. 0.5 g of Nigella sativa powder was weighed and washed twice using DI water. The washed sample was added to 50 ml of DI water and then was probe sonicated for 30 min to achieve an even dispersion of Nigella sativa powder in DI water. After being placed in a Teflon cap, the sample was put into a hydrothermal cell and heated for 8 h. The obtained solution was cooled naturally. The hydrothermal temperature was varied between 160 °C, 200 °C, and 240 °C, forming samples labeled CD-160, CD-200 and CD-240, respectively. The prepared samples undergo several filtration processes to obtain clear CDs. Firstly, the filter paper was used to remove residue and a 23 μm filter was used to further filter the solution. Finally, the membrane dialysis was conducted on the samples for 48 h to further remove impurities. The powdered CDs were obtained by freeze-drying for about 60 h.

X-ray diffractometer (XRD) 6100F from Shimadzu Company was used to examine the structural characteristics of CDs. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) with model number JEM-2100F was used to ascertain the morphology of the obtained CDs. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) microscope (Bruker Company, Vertex 70 with KBr as the reference) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with a K-ALPHA and monochromatic X-ray radiation of −10–1350 eV (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) were used further to confirm the chemical composition of CDs. The optical properties of CDs were measured using a spectrofluorometer (Hitachi-2700), and UV–vis spectroscopy (Hitachi U-3900).

A powerful CO2 laser engraving device (Versa LASER, VLS 3.50) with a wavelength of 10.6 m was used to produce finger-like designs of laser-induced graphene (LIG) in a single step. The machine was run at a power of 4.5 W and a voltage of 220 V, and Kapton paper was used for the patterning. The patterned LIG serves as a current collector. This stands on the basis of the use of LIG as a current collector reported by P. Anagbonu et al. [35]. The patterned LIG on Kapton paper is presented in Fig. 2a. A 2 mg aqueous solution of each sample of CDs was deposited on the patterned LIG using a drop-casting technique. The obtained samples were allowed to dry at room temperature (shown in Fig. 2b).

The electrochemical performance of the active area of LIG and LIG + CDs (CD-160, CD-200 and CD-240) were measured. With the help of VersaSTAT4 potentiostat, the three independent methods — Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS), Galvanostatic Charge-Discharge (GCD) and Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) — were used to evaluate the performance of the developed supercapacitors. At various potential settings, the EIS measurements were performed on the cell. The EIS measurements were carried out with an amplitude of 10 mV and frequencies which ranged from 0.1 Hz to 100 MHz.

PVA/H2SO4 gel electrolyte was used for this experiment. The following procedure was used to prepare the PVA/H2SO4 gel electrolyte [36]: 1 g of PVA powder was dissolved in 10 ml of DI water and 0.56 ml of H2SO4 at 90 °C. To produce a homogeneous solution, the liquid was vigorously stirred. The mixture was then cooled at room temperature. The prepared PVA/H2SO4 gel electrolyte was dropped cast on already prepared samples for electrochemical measurement.

Fig. 3 depicts the XRD pattern of CDs synthesized at different temperatures. All the samples showed a broad peak with the peak maximum situated around 25.5° 2θ angle which corresponds to the (002) crystallographic plane. The interlayer spacing for CD-160, CD-200, and CD-240 are 3.43, 3.53, and 3.48 Å, respectively, which is approximately equal to 3.44 Å interlayer spacing in bulk graphite [37].

The TEM images confirming the presence of synthesized CDs are shown in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4a shows the TEM image of CD-160 synthesized at a temperature of 160 °C. The image shows dots with an arrangement of leaf-like structures. The dot's average size is estimated to be 9.91 nm in diameter. When the synthesis temperature is increased to 200 °C, the dot formation starts to be more pronounced, with a cluster of them forming at a particular point and the estimated average size is 7.38 nm in diameter (shown in Fig. 4b). At 240 °C, the dots are seen to be distinct, and well-spaced, indicating a precise quasi-spherical shaped dot size distribution (shown in Fig. 4c). The average size distribution of the formed dot in CD-240 was estimated to be 6.08 nm in diameter (shown in Fig. 4d). However, going by the TEM images below, we confirmed that temperature plays a significant role in the dot formation. At low-temperature synthesis, the dot starts to form, and becomes more distinct at higher temperatures.

The FTIR spectra of CDs are shown in Fig. 5 . It was found that the characteristic peaks at 3266 cm-1 in the samples were brought on by the stretching of the NH and OH bonds. The vibrational bands seen at 2925 cm−1 were related to the stretching motions of CH bonds. While the extending movements of the CON bonds produced the peak at 1654 cm−1. The CO functional group is responsible for the absorption band at 1541 cm−1. Additionally, the peaks at 1401 cm−1 and 1063 cm−1 were connected to the vibrations of the CH and CN bonds. Similar vibrational bands have been reported for CDs [38,39]. These observed vibrational bands confirm the phytochemical content of Nigella sativa seeds such as carbonyl, hydroxyl, and mine.

The functional groups indicated by the FTIR analysis were confirmed using XPS spectra of CD-240, as shown in Fig. 6 . The survey spectra (Fig. 6a) revealed three distinct elements: C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s. The C 1s has a peak at 285.14 eV, whereas the peaks of N 1s and O 1s are 399.96 and 532 eV, respectively. The percentage of N 1s, O 1s and C 1s content in the sample is 7.6, 20.08 and 72.32 %, respectively. The percentage of O 1s and N 1s in the sample is very significant, making CDs derived from Nigella Sativa seeds behave more like a self-doped material. This self-doped nitrogen and oxygen contribute greatly to the performance of CDs-based SCs. The breakdown of C 1s spectra (shown in Fig. 6b) shows three bonding: CC at peak 284.73 eV, CN/CO at a binding energy of 285.05 eV and CO at a binding energy of 287.84 eV. Fig. 6c shows the O 1s bonding spectra plot. CO and CO are the two major bonding observed at 532.25 and 530.97 eV, respectively. The bonding of carbon materials with oxygen enhances the electrolyte surface adsorption, which results in an improved SC performance [40]. Fig. 6d shows the Pyrrolynic N and Pyridnic N peaks at 399.78 and 401.67 eV. The presence of Pyrrolynic N and Pyridnic N results in an improvement of the electrical conductivity and capacitance behavior of CD materials [41].

Understanding the stability, behavior, and interactions of colloidal dispersions depends critically on the zeta potential. It serves as a gauge of the electrokinetic potential at the point where a particle and the solvent meet. The zeta potential values of the synthesized CDs are presented in Table 1 . CD-160, CD-200, and CD-240 have Zeta potential values of −14.3, −13.7 and −10.8 mV, respectively. All the samples showed a moderate surface charge. It implies that the colloidal system is somewhat stable and that there are some repulsive forces at work between the particles. The negative sign indicates that the particles at the shear plane are negatively charged [18]. Similar moderate zeta potential value of CDs has been reported by Y. Zhou et al. [42], and S. Mondal et al. [43].

Fig. 7a–c represents the PL emission plots of CDs within 300 to 550 nm. The samples demonstrated a typical emission of a CD which red shift as the excitation wavelength increases. They also showed maximum emission at 320 nm excitation wavelength. When illuminated with UV lamp of 400 nm, the samples emit green fluorescence, with the CD-240 sample having the highest fluorescence (Fig. 7d). This green fluorescent emission aids in the application of long-wavelength micro-LEDs with a green to red spectrum [44]. The development of high bandwidth green micro-LEDs is essential because green light's low transmission loss makes it a perfect option for underwater communication. CDs have been reported to emit a similar green fluorescence [45,46]. Fig. 7e shows the absorbance plot of the synthesized CDs. The shallow peaks at 270 nm represent the π- π* electronic transition of sp2 CC bonds of aromatic rings, while the n- π* transition of fewer CO bonds was observed at 325 nm.

The CV plots of LIG and LIG + CDs are shown in Fig. 8a–d. It is observed that all the samples exhibited the electrical double layer (EDL) capacitance behavior. EDL is an area at the boundary of two distinct phases that is often thought to be comprised of two layers with opposite charges [9]. For example, a coating of positively charged ions from CD may adhere to colloidal particles, attracting a layer of negatively charged ions from PVA/H2SO4, the electrolytic solution surrounding it. These CV curves depict the electrode's performance at various scan rates. The CV curves widen as the scan rate increases from 10 to 100 mV/s. A higher scan rate encourages the adsorption of charges, which causes a stronger current response, resulting in a larger CV profile [47]. As shown in Fig. 8a–d, at 10 mV/s scan rate, the areal capacitance of LIG, LIG + CD-160, LIG + CD-200 and LIG + CD-240 are 0.49, 1.29, 1.39 and 1.57 mF/cm2, respectively. The comparison of the CV curves at 100 mV/s scan rate of all the samples is shown in Fig. 8e. The area under CV rectangular shape of LIG and LIG + CDs is shown in Fig. 8f. It was observed that all CD samples have higher areas compared to the LIG. The area under the CV curve has a strong correlation with the energy storage capacity of SCs [48]. The larger surface area of an SC CV demonstrates its capacity to store more energy, which is an essential benefit for applications demanding higher energy storage.

The mechanism of the surface charge storage process of LIG and LIG + CDs were depicted by using the following Eqs. [49].
(1)
i p
=
av b

Eq. (1) can be modified to be
(2)
log
i = log
a + blogv
where a and b are constants that can be determined from the plot of logip Vs. logv, ip is the maximum current at a fixed voltage and v is the scan rate. The linear fit's slope on the logip Vs logv graph indicates the charge storage mechanism, represented by the letter b. Surface capacitive (EDLC) coordination dominates the mechanism when b = 1. The mechanism changes to a diffusion-coordinated process when b gets closer to 0.5 [50]. Fig. 9 shows the plot of log i Vs. log v. The obtained b values for LIG, LIG + CD-160, LIG + CD-200 and LIG + CD-240 are 0.92, 0.71, 0.71, and 0.66, respectively. The value shown by LIG indicates that it has EDL capacitance mechanism and LIG + CDs have a trace of a pseudo (faradaic) capacitance mechanism resulting in a diffusion-coordinated process. The pseudo behavior seen in the LIG + CDs is attributed to the reach functional groups of CDs, resulting in excellent electrochemical performance [51].

Fig. 10a–d depicts the GCD plots of LIG and LIG + CDs based SCs. These curves were obtained using a consistent potential window of 0–0.9 V. The GCD plots in Fig. 10b–d, show that LIG + CDs have better performance when compared to LIG based SCs. A GCD curve shows how an SC responds to a steady electric current throughout the charging and discharging processes. The initial charging stage, the relaxation stage, and the discharging stage are the three sections that are most frequently visible. The charging stage frequently presents challenges, whereas the discharging stage is typically simple. The major challenge associated with the charging phase is the sluggish profile which may be attributed to high equivalent series resistance, degraded electrolyte and voltage limitation [52]. The sluggish charging in the GCD profile of LIG + CD-240 (shown in Fig. 10e) could be due to high equivalent series resistance.

The discharge curves of LIG + CDs are substantially symmetrical, demonstrating that the LIG + CDs have good electrochemical capacitance performance. At the commencement of the discharge process, all samples show a considerable fall in voltage. This voltage drop is caused by the limited dispersion of PVA/H2SO4 gel electrolyte ions within the electrode pores. In addition, the resistance at the interface between the active layer of the distinct porous CD materials, and the current collector may also be the cause of voltage drop [53]. However, zero or very low voltage drop is always good for an SC performance. The voltage drops for LIG, LIG + CD-160, LIG + CD-200 and LIG + CD-240 are 0.057, 0.074, 0.072 and 0.074 V respectively. The areal capacitance (C), volumetric capacitance (Cv), Energy density (E) and power density (P) of both LIG and LIG + CDs were calculated using the following equations [54,55].
(3)
C =
I ∆ t
A act
∆ V
(4)
C v
=
I ∆ t
V x
∆ V
(5)
E =
C
V 2
2 S
(6)
P =
E
∆ t
where I is the current, S is the scan rate
A act
is the active area (0.25 cm2),
V x
=
A act
× electrode thickness (351 nm), Δt and ΔV represent the change in elapsed time and potential window respectively.

The computed areal capacitance and volumetric capacitance is shown in Fig. 11 . The area capacitance at a current density of 5.21 μA/cm2 for LIG, LIG + CD-160, LIG + CD-200 and LIG + CD-240 are 0.87, 1.67, 2.26, 2.82 mF/cm2, respectively. At the same current density, LIG, LIG + CD-160, LIG + CD-200 and LIG showed volumetric capacitance of 24.78, 47.7, 64.51 and 80.51 F/cm3. It is worth observing that the areal capacitance and volumetric capacitance increases as the hydrothermal temperature increases. This increase could be due to the fragmentation (size reduction) of CDs as the synthesis temperature increases, resulting in improved electronic properties. The following reasons give an insight on why the capacitance is size-dependent: (i) smaller CD size results in shorter ion diffusion pathways, which accelerates charge transfer kinetics and enhances capacitive behavior dependent this is due to lesser void observed in small particles arrangement [56,57]. Larger particles have slower kinetics and may have limited overall electrochemical performance due to their longer diffusion distance for ions to go through the electrode material [58]. Ions can get to the active areas of electrochemical reactions more quickly with smaller particles because they have a shorter distance to traverse. Faster and more effective charge transfer is encouraged by this phenomenon, which improves electrochemical performance in energy storage applications by increasing specific capacitance and rate capability. (ii) Reduced particle size in CDs lessens the likelihood of agglomeration or clustering, which can impair electrochemical performance. Agglomeration reduces the accessibility of the active material and slows down the kinetics of charge transfer by causing an uneven distribution of active sites and blocking ion diffusion channels inside the electrode material. CDs show superior dispersion within the electrode matrix by preserving smaller particle sizes, which guarantees higher active material use and improves electrochemical performance. (iii) There are more active sites for charge storage in smaller size CDs due to their higher surface area-to-volume ratio [59]. This makes it possible for the electrolyte to interact with it more effectively, improving charge storage capacity.

The energy and power densities are also used to measure the SC performances. The Energy density of LIG, LIG + CD-160, LIG + CD-200 and LIG + CD-240 were found to be 2.2291 × 10−5, 3.6060 × 10−5, 5.7845 × 10−5, and 7.9023 × 10−5 mWh/cm2 respectively. On the other hand, the power density of LIG, LIG + CD-160, LIG + CD-200 and LIG + CD-240 are 3.371 × 10−3, 4.587 × 10−3, 3.602 × 10−3, 3.657 × 10−3 mW/cm2 respectively. The low energy density value shown by the CD based SCs are of merit because SCs are better suitable for applications needing frequent charge/discharge cycles and short-term energy storage than those requiring long-term energy storage [53]. Table 2 present the comparative properties of the fabricated devices while the features of the best performing sample LIG + CD-240 based SC compared with other carbon-based SCs is shown in Table 3 . One can easily infer that the CD synthesized from Nigella sativa outperforms some carbon-based electrodes synthesized from other organic materials e.g. Eichhornia crassipes and Nanodiamond powder.

The EIS measurements were carried out with an amplitude of 10 mV and frequencies which ranged from 0.1 Hz to 100 MHz. Fig. 12a shows the Nyquist plot of LIG and LIG + CD SCs. All the samples have non-faradaic behavior, which signifies the absence of the semicircle on the Nyquist plots, confirming the findings of the CV analysis, i.e., EDL capacitance behavior. The imaginary part on the curve shows a phase angle of approximately 45°, which is due to medium frequency, and it is often termed as a Warburg area/region [65,66] (A system consisting of both capacitive and resistive elements). This angle lies halfway between the 0° (pure resistive) and the 90° (pure capacitive) angles [62]. It suggests that the system exhibits a combination of resistive and capacitive. The phase angle of 45° is widely used to identify a semi-dominant capacitive behavior. Similar phase angle for carbon-based SC has been reported by P. Anagbonu et al. [35] and X. Jiang et al. [67].

In addition, EIS spectrum analyzer was used to further fit the EIS plot. The fitted plots are shown in Fig. 12b–e. The equivalent series resistance (RESR) for LIG, LIG + CD-160, LIG + CD-200, and LIG + CD-240 are 906.71, 1003.9, 1002.9, and 1162.7 Ω, respectively. The charge transfer resistance (Rct), Warburg impedance (Wo), and constant phase element (CPE 1) make up the circuit's parallel configuration. Together, these components show how the charges are transferred in the pores of CDs-based SCs. The Rct of LIG, LIG + CD-160, LIG + CD-200 and LIG + CD-240 are 5.51 × 10−8, 1.7 × 10−7, 1.3 × 10−4, 1.10 × 10−4 Ω, respectively. With a low Rct value, our supercapacitor may be charged and discharged more quickly due to the quick flow of charges within the electrodes. The fast charge/discharge feature makes it appropriate for the application needing high power delivery [68].

A hydrothermal technique was used to synthesize CD from Nigella sativa seeds. The seeds were used because they contain naturally occurring organic compounds such as carbohydrates (e.g., starch and sugar), lipids (fat and oil), proteins, and other organic components like alkaloids, flavonoids, and terpenoids. These substances are made of carbon-based complex organic molecules bound to other elements like oxygen, nitrogen, hydrogen, and sulfur, which create an environment that enables the formation of CDs. The hydrothermal temperature was varied between 160, 200, and 240 °C, forming CD-160, CD-200, and CD-240. The structural, size distribution and fluorescence properties of the synthesized CD were studied using an X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectroscope, Transition electron microscope (TEM), and spectrofluorometer respectively. The XRD showed that the interlayer spacing for CD-160, CD-200, CD-240 are 3.43, 3.53, and 3.48 Å respectively. The TEM revealed distinct, uniformly scattered spherical dot particles on the surface with an estimated size distribution of 9.55 nm. The spectrofluorometer showed a green light emission which redshifts as the excitation wavelength increases and the maximum emission was seen at 320 nm excitation wavelength. The electrochemical measurement confirms that CD exhibited an electrical double layer (EDL) capacitance behavior and the areal capacitance of CD-160, CD-200 and CD-240 are 0.81, 1.39, 1.95 mF/cm2, respectively, at 5.21 μA/cm2 current density.

Cyril O. Ugwuoke: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization. Mohsen Ghali: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Ahmed Abd El-Moneim: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: C.O.U acknowledges JICA through the TICAD7 Scholarship. This work was supported by project 38311 from the STDF agency in Egypt.

AIP Conf. Proc.

J. Mater. Chem. A
